exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Jerk Balance exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Kneeling High Pulley Row
instructions: Select the appropriate weight using a pulley that is above your head. Attach a rope to the cable and kneel a couple of feet away, holding the rope out in front of you with both arms extended. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by flexing the elbows and fully retracting your shoulders, pulling the rope toward your upper chest with your elbows out.
After pausing briefly, slowly return to the starting position.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the back region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Kneeling High Pulley Row Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Kneeling High Pulley Row exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Low Pulley Row To Neck
instructions: Sit on a low pulley row machine with a rope attachment.

Grab the ends of the rope using a palms-down grip and sit with your back straight and your knees slightly bent. Tip: Keep your back almost completely vertical and your arms fully extended in front of you. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your torso stationary, lift your elbows and start bending them as you pull the rope towards your neck while exhaling. Throughout the movement your upper arms should remain parallel to the floor. Tip: Continue this motion until your hands are almost next to your ears (the forearms will not be parallel to the floor at the end of the movement as they will be angled a bit upwards) and your elbows are out away from your sides.
After holding for a second or so at the contracted position, come back slowly to the starting position as you inhale. Tip: Again, during no part of the movement should the torso move.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Low Pulley Row To Neck Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Low Pulley Row To Neck exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your feet as a pronated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your face as a supinated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
Switch arms again and repeat the movement.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.

exercises.seoTitle: One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a pronated grip (palms facing down) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.

exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Push Press - Behind the Neck
instructions: Standing with the weight racked on the back of the shoulders, begin with the dip. With your feet directly under your hips, flex the knees without moving the hips backward. Go down only slightly, and reverse direction as powerfully as possible. Drive through the heels create as much speed and force as possible, moving the bar in a vertical path.
Using the momentum generated, finish pressing the weight overhead be extending through the arms.
Return to the starting position, using your legs to absorb the impact.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Push Press - Behind the Neck Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Push Press - Behind the Neck exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Rack Delivery
instructions: This drill teaches the delivery of the barbell to the rack position on the shoulders. Begin holding a bar in the scarecrow position, with the upper arms parallel to the floor, and the forearms hanging down. Use a hook grip, with your fingers wrapped over your thumbs.

Begin by rotating the elbows around the bar, delivering the bar to the shoulders. As your elbows come forward, relax your grip. The shoulders should be protracted, providing a shelf for the bar, which should lightly contact the throat.

It is important that the bar stay close to the body at all times, as with a heavier load any distance will result in an unwanted collision. As the movement becomes smoother, speed and load can be increased before progressing further.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.

exercises.seoTitle: Rack Delivery Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Rack Delivery exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Sit down at the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in one arm using a neutral grip (palms of the hand facing you).
Bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arm with the dumbbell should be close to the torso and aligned with it (lifted up until it is parallel to the floor while the forearms are pointing towards the floor as the hands hold the weights). Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearms and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arm stationary, use the triceps to lift the weight as you exhale until the forearm is parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Seated Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Sit down at the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in both arms using a neutral grip (palms of the hand facing you).
Bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arms with the dumbbells should be close to the torso and aligned with it (lifted up until they are parallel to the floor while the forearms are pointing towards the floor as the hands hold the weights). Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearms and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to lift the weight as you exhale until the forearms are parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Seated Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Inner Biceps Curl
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
Rotate the palms of the hands so that they are facing inward in a neutral position. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the dumbbells out and up, turning the palms out as you lift and keeping your forearms in line with your outer deltoids. Tips:

Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to the starting position as your breathe in. Remember to rotate your arms as you lower the dumbbells so that you can switch back to a neutral grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on the floor in front of a flat bench.
Sit down on the edge of the flat bench with your legs at about shoulder width apart. Make sure to keep your feet on the floor.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against your thighs with the palms of the hands facing down. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge of your thighs.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.

Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise. Tip: Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When finished, simply lower the dumbbells to the floor.

exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on the floor in front of a flat bench.
Sit down on the edge of the flat bench with your legs at about shoulder width apart. Make sure to keep your feet on the floor.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against your thighs with the palms of the hands facing up. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge of your thighs.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.

Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise. Tip: Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in your right hand.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.

Lean forward and place your right forearm on top of your upper right thigh with your palm down. Tip: Make sure that the back of the wrist lies on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the dumbbell as far as possible as you keep a tight grip on the dumbbell. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Now curl the dumbbell as high as possible as you contract the forearms and as you exhale. Keep the contraction for a second before you lower again. Tip: The only movement should happen at the wrist.
Perform for the recommended amount of repetitions, switch arms and repeat the movement.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.

benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.

exercises.name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in your right hand.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart.

Lean forward and place your right forearm on top of your upper right thigh with your palm up. Tip: Make sure that the front of the wrist lies on top of your knees. This will be your starting position.
Lower the dumbbell as far as possible as you keep a tight grip on the dumbbell. Inhale as you perform this movement.
Now curl the dumbbell as high as possible as you contract the forearms and as you exhale. Keep the contraction for a second before you lower again. Tip: The only movement should happen at the wrist.
Perform for the recommended amount of repetitions, switch arms and repeat the movement.

exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Seated Side Lateral Raise
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and sit at the end of a flat bench with your feet firmly on the floor. Hold the dumbbells with your palms facing in and your arms straight down at your sides at arms' length. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbells to your side with a slight bend on the elbow and the hands slightly tilted forward as if pouring water in a glass. Continue to go up until you arms are parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this movement and pause for a second at the top.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.

exercises.seoTitle: Seated Side Lateral Raise Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Seated Side Lateral Raise exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Standing Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: With a dumbbell in one hand and the palm facing your torso, bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Make sure that you keep the head up.

The upper arm should be close to the torso and parallel to the floor while the forearm is pointing towards the floor as the hand holds the weight. Tip: There should be a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm. This is your starting position.

Keeping the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to lift the weights as you exhale until the forearms are parallel to the floor and the whole arm is extended. Like many other arm exercises, only the forearm moves.
After a second contraction at the top, slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Standing Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Standing Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Standing Inner-Biceps Curl
instructions: Stand up with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso. Your legs should be at about shoulder's width apart from each other.
Rotate the palms of the hands so that they are facing inward in a neutral position. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights out while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Your wrist should turn so that when the weights are fully elevated you have supinated grip (palms facing up).
Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Tip: Keep the forearms aligned with your outer deltoids.
Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to the starting position as your breathe in. Remember to rotate the wrists as you lower the weight in order to switch back to a neutral grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Standing Inner-Biceps Curl Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Standing Inner-Biceps Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Curl Over Incline Bench
instructions: Stand on the back side of an incline bench as if you were going to be a spotter for someone. Have a dumbbell in one hand and rest it across the incline bench with a supinated (palms up) grip.

Position your non lifting hand at the corner or side of the incline bench. The chest should be pressed against the top part of the incline and your feet should be pressed against the floor at a wide stance. This is the starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the dumbbell upward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms while performing this exercise.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Curl Over Incline Bench Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Curl Over Incline Bench exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: To begin, stand up with a dumbbell held in one hand. Your feet should be about shoulder width apart from each other. Now fully extend the arm with the dumbbell over your head. Tip: The small finger of your hand should be facing the ceiling and the palm of your hand should be facing forward. The dumbbell should be above your head.
This will be your starting position.

Keeping your upper arm close to your head (elbows in) and perpendicular to the floor, lower the resistance in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearm touch your bicep. Tip: The upper arm should remain stationary and only the forearm should move. Breathe in as you perform this step.
Go back to the starting position by using the triceps to raise the dumbbell. Breathe out as you perform this step.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Zottman Curl
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
Make sure the palms of the hands are facing each other. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Your wrist should rotate so that you have a supinated (palms up) grip. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level.
Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Now during the contracted position, rotate your wrist until you now have a pronated (palms facing down) grip with the thumb at a higher position than the pinky.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back down using the pronated grip.
As the dumbbells close your thighs, start rotating the wrist so that you go back to a neutral (palms facing your body) grip.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Zottman Curl Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Zottman Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Zottman Preacher Curl
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and place your upper arms on top of the preacher bench or the incline bench. The dumbbells should be held at shoulder height and the elbows should be flexed. Hold the dumbbells with the palms of your hands facing down. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the dumbbells keeping the palms down until your upper arm is extended and your biceps are fully stretched.
Now rotate your wrists once you are at the bottom of the movement so that the palms of the hands are facing up.

As you exhale, use your biceps to curl the weights up until they are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder height. Again, remember that to ensure full contraction you need to bring that small finger higher than the thumb.
Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position and rotate your wrists so that the palms are facing down again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Zottman Preacher Curl Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Zottman Preacher Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Alternate Incline Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Sit down on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand being held at arms length. Tip: Keep the elbows close to the torso.This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the right weight forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. As you do so, rotate the hand so that the palm is facing up. Continue the movement until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbell back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat the movement with the left hand. This equals one repetition.
Continue alternating in this manner for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Alternate Incline Dumbbell Curl Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Alternate Incline Dumbbell Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Alternating Cable Shoulder Press
instructions: Move the cables to the bottom of the tower and select an appropriate weight.
Grasp the cables and hold them at shoulder height, palms facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your head and chest up, extend through the elbow to press one side directly over head.
After pausing at the top, return to the starting position and repeat on the opposite side.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Alternating Cable Shoulder Press Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Alternating Cable Shoulder Press exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Cable Hip Adduction
instructions: Stand in front of a low pulley facing forward with one leg next to the pulley and the other one away.
Attach the ankle cuff to the cable and also to the ankle of the leg that is next to the pulley.
Now step out and away from the stack with a wide stance and grasp the bar of the pulley system.
Stand on the foot that does not have the ankle cuff (the far foot) and allow the leg with the cuff to be pulled towards the low pulley. This will be your starting position.
Now perform the movement by moving the leg with the ankle cuff in front of the far leg by using the inner thighs to abduct the hip. Breathe out during this portion of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then repeat the same movement with the opposite leg.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the legs region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Cable Hip Adduction Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Cable Hip Adduction exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Cable Russian Twists
instructions: Connect a standard handle attachment, and position the cable to a middle pulley position.
Lie on a stability ball perpendicular to the cable and grab the handle with one hand. You should be approximately arm's length away from the pulley, with the tension of the weight on the cable.
Grab the handle with both hands and fully extend your arms above your chest. You hands should be directly in-line with the pulley. If not, adjust the pulley up or down until they are.
Keep your hips elevated and abs engaged. Rotate your torso away from the pulley for a full-quarter rotation. Your body should be flat from head to knees.
Pause for a moment and in a slow and controlled manner reset to the starting position. You should still have side tension on the cable in the resting position.
Repeat the same movement to failure.
Then, reposition and repeat the same series of movements on the opposite side.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the core region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Cable Russian Twists Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Cable Russian Twists exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Cable Shoulder Press
instructions: Move the cables to the bottom of the towers and select an appropriate weight.
Stand directly in between the uprights. Grasp the cables and hold them at shoulder height, palms facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your head and chest up, extend through the elbow to press the handles directly over head.
After pausing at the top, return to the starting position and repeat.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Cable Shoulder Press Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Cable Shoulder Press exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

Once you are laying down, move the dumbbells in front of you at shoulder width. The palms of the hands should be facing each other and the arms should be perpendicular to the floor and fully extended. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary (and elbows in), bring the dumbbells down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until your thumbs are next to your ears. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the dumbbells back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand (torso upright) with a dumbbell in each hand held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso and the palms of your hand should be facing your thighs.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the right weight as you rotate the palm of the hands until they are facing forward. At this point continue contracting the biceps as you breathe out until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbell back to the starting position as your breathe in. Tip: Remember to twist the palms back to the starting position (facing your thighs) as you come down.
Repeat the movement with the left hand. This equals one repetition.
Continue alternating in this manner for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Dumbbell Alternate Bicep Curl Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Dumbbell Alternate Bicep Curl exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Scaption
instructions: This corrective exercise strengthens the muscles that stabilize your shoulder blade. Hold a light weight in each hand, hanging at your sides. Your thumbs should pointing up.
Begin the movement raising your arms out in front of you, about 30 degrees off center. Your arms should be fully extended as you perform the movement.
Continue until your arms are parallel to the ground, and then return to the starting position.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the shoulders region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.
exercises.seoTitle: Dumbbell Scaption Exercise: Muscles Worked, Tips, and How To Do It
exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Dumbbell Scaption exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Full Range-Of-Motion Lat Pulldown
instructions: Either standing or seated on a high bench, grasp two stirrup cables that are attached to the high pulleys. Grab with the opposing hand so your arms are crisscrossed about you and your palms are facing forward.

Keeping your chest up and maintaining a slight arch in your lower back, pull the handles down as if you were doing a regular pulldown. The range of motion will be more of an arc. During the movement, rotate your hands so that in the bottom position your palms face each other rather than forward. Return slowly to the starting position and repeat.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Letting momentum do the work instead of controlling the full rep.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the back region.
Trains multiple joints at once, which can make your sessions more efficient.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.

exercises.seoDescription: Learn how to do the Full Range-Of-Motion Lat Pulldown exercise, which muscles it works, the equipment you need, and common mistakes to avoid.

exercises.name: Hammer Curls
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell on each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
The palms of the hands should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

Now, while holding your upper arm stationary, exhale and curl the weight forward while contracting the biceps. Continue to raise the weight until the biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a brief moment as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Focus on keeping the elbow stationary and only moving your forearm.
After the brief pause, inhale and slowly begin the lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
commonMistakes: Using more weight or speed than you can control cleanly.
Cutting the range of motion short and rushing through the reps.
Changing your body position between reps instead of keeping the movement repeatable.
benefits: Builds strength and control through the arms region.
Makes it easier to focus on one area when you want extra practice or volume.
Gives you a repeatable way to track progress inside Logbook over time.

workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone new to lifting who wants a clear first program with a little bit of everything.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Start with 5 minutes of light cardio, then do one easy warm-up set before each main lift.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: When every set feels smooth at the top of the rep range, add a small amount of weight the next session.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Full Body Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Full Body Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Beginner Weight Loss Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A low-complexity circuit that keeps you moving while still building basic strength.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: A beginner who wants a sustainable starting point for fat loss instead of an extreme plan.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Walk for 5 minutes and practice one slow rehearsal set of each movement before the circuit starts.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add a round, a few reps, or a small amount of load before you jump straight to much harder exercises.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Weight Loss Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Weight Loss Workout is a beginner workout template focused on weight loss with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Beginner Dumbbell Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A full-body dumbbell session that works well in a home gym or a crowded commercial gym.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who only has dumbbells or wants to learn the basics before using barbells.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Take one lighter warm-up set for the first lower-body and upper-body move.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Own the full rep range first, then increase weight in the smallest jump available.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Dumbbell Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Dumbbell Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Beginner Gym Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A beginner-friendly gym session built around stable machines and simple cable patterns.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone brand new to the gym floor who wants a little more support and stability.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use 5 minutes of easy cardio and one lighter warm-up set on the first two machines.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Only move the pin up after you hit every prescribed rep without grinding.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Gym Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Gym Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Beginner Home Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A bodyweight-only starting plan you can run at home with very little setup.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants a realistic at-home plan before buying more equipment.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Walk around for a few minutes, then do a slow practice round of the circuit.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add reps first. When that gets easy, shorten rest periods or add a fourth round.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Home Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Home Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Push Day Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A classic push day for chest, shoulders, and triceps with a balance of compound and accessory work.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who already know the basics and want a clean upper-body push session.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up your shoulders and upper back, then climb in weight with 2 to 3 ramp-up sets on the first press.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Drive progress through the first two presses first, then add reps or load on accessories as recovery allows.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Push Day Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Push Day Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Pull Day Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A pull day built around back thickness, back width, and direct arm work.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want a simple back and biceps session they can progress for months.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Do a few shoulder circles, a couple sets of band or cable rows, and then ramp into the first rowing movement.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Chase better form and load on the main row before making the accessory work more complicated.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Pull Day Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Pull Day Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Leg Day Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A leg day that covers squat strength, posterior-chain work, and enough accessory volume to grow.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants a balanced lower-body session instead of only squats and leg press.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up the hips and ankles, then build into the first squat pattern with 3 to 4 ramp-up sets.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Treat the squat and hinge as the main drivers of progress, then use the accessories to add controlled volume.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Leg Day Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Leg Day Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Upper Body Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A balanced upper-body day built around pressing, rowing, pulling, and arm accessories.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want a straightforward upper session they can run once or twice per week.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Prime the upper back and shoulders first, then take a few ramp-up sets on the main press.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Progress the first press and row as a pair, then add small improvements on the rest of the session.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Upper Body Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Upper Body Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Lower Body Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A lower-body template that covers squat strength, single-leg work, and posterior-chain volume.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want a lower day that is productive without being overly complicated.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Mobilize hips and ankles, then build into the first squat movement with several small jumps.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add weight slowly on the main squat pattern and use the accessories to own more reps before more load.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Lower Body Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Lower Body Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: 3-Day Full Body Split
workoutTemplates.overview: A three-day split that spreads total weekly work across three full-body sessions.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who can train three days per week and wants steady progress without body-part specialization yet.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use 5 minutes of easy cardio plus one to three ramp-up sets before the first compound lift on each day.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Aim to improve one big lift and one accessory on each day before adding more overall volume.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: 3-Day Full Body Split: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: 3-Day Full Body Split is a beginner workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: 4-Day Upper Lower Split
workoutTemplates.overview: A simple four-day split that gives each half of the body two quality sessions each week.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone beyond the beginner stage who wants structure without a five- or six-day schedule.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up separately for the first press or squat on each day, then take one lighter set before the next big lift.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Rotate your focus between load on the first compounds and rep quality on the secondary work.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: 4-Day Upper Lower Split: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: 4-Day Upper Lower Split is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: 5-Day Bodybuilding Split
workoutTemplates.overview: A traditional five-day bodybuilding split for lifters who enjoy focusing on one or two regions per session.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who can recover from higher weekly volume and likes dedicated body-part days.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use targeted mobility and ramp-up sets for the first compound on each day.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Keep the main compound stable from block to block and rotate smaller accessories as needed.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: 5-Day Bodybuilding Split: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: 5-Day Bodybuilding Split is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Chest and Triceps Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A chest-focused session with enough triceps work to support pressing strength and arm size.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want to bias chest volume without turning the workout into random burnout work.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Open the shoulders and upper back first, then use 2 to 3 ramp-up sets on the first bench pattern.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Improve the first press over time, then use the cable and triceps work to build extra volume.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Chest and Triceps Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Chest and Triceps Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Back and Biceps Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A straightforward back and biceps workout with vertical pulls, rows, and direct arm work.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want a dedicated pull session with a little more direct biceps volume.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use easy rows and shoulder circles before you start ramping into the first heavy pull.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Keep the first row or pulldown movement stable long enough to actually see strength move.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Back and Biceps Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Back and Biceps Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Shoulder Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A shoulder session built around pressing and higher-rep delt work.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want more direct shoulder work without adding too much unnecessary complexity.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up the rotator cuff, upper back, and thoracic spine before the main press.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Push the main press over time and keep the lateral and rear-delt work strict.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Shoulder Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Shoulder Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Arm Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A focused arm session with direct biceps and triceps volume you can recover from.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Lifters who want a shorter accessory-focused session to bring up the arms.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up elbows and shoulders with a couple of very light cable or dumbbell sets.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Progress slowly and stay honest with form so the target muscles keep doing the work.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Arm Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Arm Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Glute Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A glute-focused lower-body session that still keeps the rest of the lower body working.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants more direct glute emphasis without abandoning overall leg strength.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Do a few glute bridges and bodyweight hinges before you start adding load.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Drive the hip-dominant lifts first, then use the single-leg work and accessories for extra volume.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Glute Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Glute Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Core Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A direct core session built around control, trunk stiffness, and consistent tension.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Anyone who wants a simple core-focused add-on without turning it into endless random ab work.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Take a minute to breathe and brace before you start the first set.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Progress by owning longer holds or cleaner reps before you chase harder variations.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Core Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Core Workout is a beginner workout template focused on muscle building with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: 30-Minute Fat Loss Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A shorter full-body session designed to keep you moving and make consistency easier on busy days.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants a time-efficient workout instead of skipping the session altogether.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Walk for a few minutes, then do one lighter round before the work sets start.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add reps or reduce rest before you add much more load.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: 30-Minute Fat Loss Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: 30-Minute Fat Loss Workout is a beginner workout template focused on weight loss with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Walking and Strength Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A lighter strength day built to pair well with a daily walking routine.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone using walking as their main activity base and lifting to keep strength moving forward.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use a few minutes of easy walking before the first strength exercise, then save the rest of the walk for the end.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Improve the strength work slowly and increase walking time only when recovery still feels good.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Walking and Strength Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Walking and Strength Workout is a beginner workout template focused on weight loss with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Low-Impact Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A gentle full-body workout that stays lower impact while still training the main muscle groups.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone easing back into training, managing joint irritation, or wanting a lower-impact option.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Move through a gentle range of motion and keep the first set of each move very easy.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add reps or another round first. Do not rush to turn a low-impact session into a max-effort one.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Low-Impact Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Low-Impact Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Hotel Gym Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A simple travel workout built around the dumbbells and bench you can usually find in a hotel gym.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone traveling who wants to keep the training habit alive without chasing a perfect setup.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use a few minutes of walking and one easy set of each first movement.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Keep the goal simple: do the work well and stay consistent while traveling.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Hotel Gym Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Hotel Gym Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Busy Schedule 3-Day Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A three-day plan for people who need productive sessions without long training windows.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: A busy adult who wants to stay consistent with three manageable lifting days each week.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Keep warm-ups short but intentional with a few easy ramp-up sets for the first lift.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: If life gets hectic, focus on the first three movements and consider the rest a bonus.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Busy Schedule 3-Day Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Busy Schedule 3-Day Workout is a beginner workout template focused on beginner with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Beginner Strength Program
workoutTemplates.overview: A simple strength-first plan that keeps the exercise menu small enough to progress consistently.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants to learn barbell basics and make steady progress on the main lifts.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use multiple small jumps in load and keep the warm-up reps crisp before your work sets begin.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Add small amounts of weight only when the prescribed reps are still clean and repeatable.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Beginner Strength Program: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Beginner Strength Program is a beginner workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Powerlifting Beginner Program
workoutTemplates.overview: A beginner-friendly powerlifting structure built around the squat, bench press, and deadlift.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone interested in strength sports who wants a clear first step without overly advanced programming.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Treat the first three compound lifts seriously and take enough warm-up sets to feel sharp before work sets.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Think long term. Small jumps and clean technique matter more than testing maxes too early.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Powerlifting Beginner Program: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Powerlifting Beginner Program is a beginner workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Squat-Focused Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A lower-body workout that biases squat strength while still supporting long-term lower-body development.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants extra squat practice without turning the whole week into pure fatigue.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Spend time opening the ankles and hips, then ramp up to your work sets gradually.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Keep the first squat pattern as the main driver and let the rest of the day support it.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Squat-Focused Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Squat-Focused Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Bench Press-Focused Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: An upper-body session designed to put the bench press first while still keeping shoulders and triceps moving forward.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone trying to improve bench strength without losing chest and triceps size work.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Use upper-back activation and several bench ramp-up sets before the work sets begin.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Keep the first bench pattern consistent long enough to measure actual progress instead of hopping around.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Bench Press-Focused Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Bench Press-Focused Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Deadlift-Focused Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A posterior-chain-heavy day built to support better deadlift performance without overcomplicating the session.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants extra deadlift attention while keeping the back and hamstrings strong enough to support it.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up the hinge pattern gradually and avoid jumping straight to heavy pulls cold.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Let the deadlift lead the day and keep the accessory work heavy enough to matter but clean enough to recover from.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Deadlift-Focused Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Deadlift-Focused Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.

workoutTemplates.name: Strength and Hypertrophy Workout
workoutTemplates.overview: A mixed approach that pairs lower-rep compound work with higher-rep accessories in the same week.
workoutTemplates.whoItIsFor: Someone who wants to keep getting stronger while still training for size and shape.
workoutTemplates.warmupGuidance: Warm up the first compound thoroughly, then keep the accessory warm-up shorter and simpler.
workoutTemplates.progressionGuidance: Use load progression on the first lift of each day and rep progression on the rest of the session.
workoutTemplates.seoTitle: Strength and Hypertrophy Workout: Free Workout Template for Logbook
workoutTemplates.seoDescription: Strength and Hypertrophy Workout is a intermediate workout template focused on strength with exercises, sets, reps, and rest guidance.
